1: Welcome. This is a calm walkthrough about recovery and the pelvic floor after birth. It is mostly for you who gave birth, but you do this together, at your own pace.
2: We will go through what happens in the body after birth, how the healing moves week by week, the discharge and the bleeding, the pelvic floor and pelvic floor exercises, tears and scars, starting to move again, sex and intimacy, warning signs, and Joakim's role. Ask whenever you like.
3: Your body has to undo nine months of change in just a few weeks. The womb shrinks back towards the size of a fist, the hormones swing and can bring sweats, tears, and mood changes, and you may feel afterpains as the womb contracts, often clearly just as you breastfeed. The deep tiredness is part of the healing, not laziness.
4: There is no fixed schedule, but this is roughly how it tends to move forward. The first days are about rest, the discharge is heavy, and everything feels tender. In week one to two the bleeding eases and you can manage a little more. Around week six it is time for the postnatal check-up, and many tears have healed by then. And over three to six months the pelvic floor and your energy are built back step by step. Don't compare your pace with anyone else's.
5: After birth you bleed from the wound where the placenta sat, this is called lochia, or the discharge. It decreases and changes colour over time. The first days it is red and heavy, then brownish-pink for a couple of weeks, and finally yellowish-white before it fades after three to six weeks. Use a pad and not a tampon for the first few weeks. A little more flow when you stand up or breastfeed is normal.
6: The pelvic floor is a hammock of muscles at the very bottom of the pelvis. It carries your organs, holds tight for wee, poo, and wind, and plays a part in desire. The weight of pregnancy and the birth itself have stretched and weakened it, so it needs time to find its way back. It can be trained, gently and step by step. Listen for signs such as leaking when you jump or cough, or a feeling of heaviness downwards.
7: You do pelvic floor exercises by squeezing as if holding back wind and drawing upwards and inwards, without tensing your tummy, bottom, or thighs, and you keep breathing the whole time. Press play for an audio-guided exercise that takes you through squeeze, hold, and release. Relaxing completely afterwards is just as important as the squeeze itself. A few rounds a couple of times a day is enough.
8: Tears and cuts usually heal in two to four weeks, and the stitches tend to dissolve on their own. Keep the area clean and dry by rinsing with lukewarm water after using the toilet, patting it dry, and changing your pad often. Ease the pain with cold, over-the-counter pain relief, and a soft cushion to sit on. If you gave birth by caesarean, keep the scar clean and dry, avoid heavy lifting, and support your tummy when you cough.
9: Starting to move again is about the right order. Rest first, the first weeks are for healing. Then short walks, ideally with the pram. Let the discharge guide you: if the bleeding increases afterwards, you have done too much. Wait with the heavy things, like running, jumping, and core training, until the pelvic floor can carry it, often after week six to eight. A physiotherapist for the pelvic floor can be a great help.
10: There is no timetable for sex after birth. You begin again when the body has healed and the desire is there, not a day before. Wait until the bleeding has stopped and tears or a section feel healed. Lubricant helps, because breastfeeding lowers oestrogen and can cause dryness. Start gently, closeness counts too. And think about contraception, because breastfeeding does not reliably protect against a new pregnancy.
11: Most of it is normal healing, but some signs should be taken seriously. A decreasing discharge and mild tenderness are normal, contact maternity care or the child health centre (BVC) with any questions. Contact care the same day if the discharge increases again or smells bad, if you feel feverish, or if you have trouble passing urine. Seek emergency care and call eleven seventy-seven or one hundred and twelve if you have large blood clots or bleeding that soaks a pad in an hour, a high fever, severe pain, or pain and swelling in one leg.
12: Joakim, you cannot heal for her, but you can make the healing much easier. Take the load at home with the housework, the food, and the nights so that she can rest. Protect the rest and keep away stress and visits that take too much energy. Be patient with the body, the bleeding, and the fact that desire takes time. And pay attention: lasting low mood or anxiety is not something to wait out, help out by getting in touch with care.
13: The body heals, step by step. Give it time, be kind to yourself, and ask for help early. Get in touch with any questions.
